title: 3.2 Pattern Matching
subtitle: BF algorithm, KMP algorithm, and applications

content.introduction: Pattern matching solves the problem of finding a pattern string inside a text string. It is fundamental in text processing, search systems, compilers, and bioinformatics.

sections.title: 3.2.1 Problem Definition and Terminology
sections.content:
Given text T (length n) and pattern P (length m), determine whether P appears in T, and if so, return occurrence positions.

Terminology:
1) Exact matching: characters must match exactly.
2) First match vs all matches: return first index or all valid indices.
3) Prefix and suffix: key concepts used in advanced algorithms.

Challenges:
- Large text sizes make naive approaches expensive.
- Repeated prefixes in pattern can be exploited to skip redundant comparisons.
examples: Search keyword in log files with millions of lines

sections.title: 3.2.2 BF (Brute Force) Algorithm
sections.content:
Core Idea:
Align pattern at each possible text position and compare characters one by one. On mismatch, shift pattern by one position and restart comparison.

Process:
1) Set i at current text start, j at pattern start.
2) If T[i + j] == P[j], continue j++.
3) If mismatch, move start to i + 1 and reset j.
4) If j reaches m, a match is found.

Complexity:
- Worst case: O(n * m)
- Best case: O(n) for early mismatches
- Space: O(1)

Advantages and Limitations:
- Very simple and easy to implement.
- Can be slow on repetitive text/pattern combinations.
examples: Pattern "aaaab" in text "aaaaaaaaab" causes many repeated comparisons
Small inputs often make BF acceptable
BF is useful as a baseline for correctness testing

sections.title: 3.2.3 KMP Algorithm
sections.content:
Core Idea:
When mismatch occurs, avoid rechecking known-equal characters by using prefix information of the pattern.

Key Preprocessing:
- Build next/LPS array (Longest Proper Prefix which is also Suffix).
- LPS tells where to continue in pattern after mismatch.

Matching Phase:
1) Compare text and pattern pointers.
2) On match, advance both pointers.
3) On mismatch, move pattern pointer using LPS instead of resetting to zero.
4) Continue until text ends or full match found.

Complexity:
- Preprocessing: O(m)
- Matching: O(n)
- Total: O(n + m), Space: O(m)

Why KMP is Better:
- Eliminates redundant comparisons in repetitive patterns.
- Provides stable linear-time performance.
examples: KMP performs well in long document search
KMP is a core technique for efficient exact matching

sections.title: 3.2.4 Applications of Pattern Matching
sections.content:
Typical Application Domains:
1) Text editor search and replace.
2) Log and monitoring system keyword detection.
3) DNA/protein sequence analysis.
4) Intrusion detection and signature scanning.
5) Compiler lexical processing and token recognition.

Engineering Considerations:
- Choose BF for simple, small-scale scenarios.
- Choose KMP when predictable linear performance is required.
- For multi-pattern or fuzzy matching, use specialized algorithms (e.g., Aho-Corasick, regex engines).
examples: IDE search panel finds symbol occurrences in source files
SIEM platform detects known malicious signatures

keyTakeaways: Pattern matching locates pattern occurrences in text efficiently
BF is simple but can degrade to O(n * m)
KMP uses prefix-suffix preprocessing to achieve O(n + m)
Algorithm choice should match data scale and performance requirements
Pattern matching is widely used across software and scientific systems
